title: Encephalitis
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Emergency Medicine, Neurology, Infectious Disease
objectives: Recognize clinical features distinguishing encephalitis from meningitis
Understand HSV encephalitis as neurologic emergency requiring immediate treatment
Apply diagnostic approach including CSF analysis and imaging
Select appropriate empiric therapy with acyclovir
Identify when to repeat HSV PCR testing

vignette.text:
A 45-year-old woman presents with 3 days of fever, headache, and progressive confusion. Family reports she has been acting strangely with personality changes and word-finding difficulty. She had a seizure in the ED.

Past Medical History: None

Medications: None

Allergies: NKDA

physicalExam.neuro: GCS 13 (E3V4M6), oriented to person only, mild word-finding difficulty, no focal motor deficits, no meningismus

questions.stem: This patient has fever, confusion, personality changes, and seizure.
questions.question: How do you distinguish encephalitis from meningitis?

options.text: ENCEPHALITIS vs MENINGITIS: MENINGITIS: Inflammation of meninges; Classic triad: Fever, headache, neck stiffness (nuchal rigidity); Mental status typically preserved early (unless severe); Kernig's/Brudzinski's signs; ENCEPHALITIS: Inflammation of brain PARENCHYMA; Key feature: ALTERED MENTAL STATUS (confusion, personality change, behavioral changes); Often NO nuchal rigidity; Focal neurologic deficits common (aphasia, hemiparesis); SEIZURES (more common than meningitis); Movement disorders possible; OVERLAP (Meningoencephalitis): Both features present; This patient: Confusion + personality change + seizure + NO meningismus = ENCEPHALITIS pattern
Meningitis always causes altered mental status
Encephalitis always has neck stiffness
Seizures are more common in meningitis

questions.explanation: Distinguishing Encephalitis from Meningitis: MENINGITIS (inflammation of meninges): Classic triad: Fever, headache, nuchal rigidity. Kernig's sign (pain with knee extension when hip flexed). Brudzinski's sign (neck flexion causes hip/knee flexion). Mental status often preserved early. Photophobia common. ENCEPHALITIS (inflammation of brain parenchyma): KEY FEATURE: Altered mental status - confusion, personality changes, behavioral abnormalities, disorientation. Often NO nuchal rigidity (not primarily meningeal). Focal neurologic deficits - aphasia, hemiparesis, ataxia. SEIZURES - much more common than in pure meningitis. Movement disorders (myoclonus, tremor). Hallucinations, psychosis possible. CLINICAL CRITERIA FOR ENCEPHALITIS: Altered mental status >24 hours without alternative cause PLUS at least two of: Documented fever ≥38°C. New focal neurologic signs. CSF pleocytosis. New abnormalities on EEG. Abnormal neuroimaging. This patient: Confusion + personality change + seizure + NO meningismus = Classic encephalitis pattern.

questions.question: What are the common causes of encephalitis?

options.text: ENCEPHALITIS ETIOLOGIES: VIRAL (most common): HSV-1: MOST COMMON sporadic cause (leading cause of fatal encephalitis); HSV-2: More common in neonates and immunocompromised; VZV: Second most common herpesvirus cause; Arboviruses: West Nile, Eastern/Western equine, St. Louis (seasonal, geographic); Enteroviruses: More common in children; CMV, EBV, HHV-6: Especially immunocompromised; Rabies: Animal exposure history; AUTOIMMUNE: NMDA receptor antibody encephalitis (young women, ovarian teratoma); Other autoantibody-mediated; INFECTIOUS (non-viral): Listeria (immunocompromised, elderly); Mycoplasma; Rickettsial (RMSF, ehrlichiosis); 37-62% remain UNKNOWN despite workup
Bacterial causes are most common
HSV-2 is the most common cause in adults
Autoimmune causes are rare

questions.explanation: Encephalitis Etiologies: VIRAL CAUSES (Most Common): HERPES SIMPLEX VIRUS: HSV-1: MOST COMMON cause of sporadic encephalitis. Leading cause of fatal sporadic encephalitis if untreated. Affects temporal lobes characteristically. HSV-2: More common in neonates and immunocompromised. VARICELLA-ZOSTER VIRUS (VZV): Second most common herpesvirus cause. Can occur without rash. ARBOVIRUSES (arthropod-borne): West Nile virus (most common arboviral cause in US). Eastern/Western equine encephalitis. St. Louis encephalitis. Seasonal (summer/fall), geographic variation. ENTEROVIRUSES: More common in children. OTHERS: CMV (immunocompromised). EBV. HHV-6 (post-transplant). Rabies (animal exposure). AUTOIMMUNE ENCEPHALITIS: NMDA receptor antibody encephalitis. Often affects young women. Associated with ovarian teratoma. Psychiatric symptoms prominent. Other autoantibody-mediated (LGI1, CASPR2, etc.). BACTERIAL/OTHER: Listeria monocytogenes. Mycoplasma pneumoniae. Rickettsial infections. UNKNOWN: 37-62% of cases have no identified etiology despite thorough workup.

questions.stem: HSV encephalitis is the most important diagnosis to consider.
questions.question: What are the characteristic features of HSV encephalitis?

options.text: HSV ENCEPHALITIS FEATURES: NEUROLOGIC EMERGENCY - 70% mortality if untreated!; CLINICAL: Acute onset fever + altered mental status; Personality/behavioral changes (temporal lobe); Aphasia, word-finding difficulty; Olfactory/gustatory hallucinations; Seizures (focal or generalized); Memory impairment; TEMPORAL LOBE PREDILECTION: Virus enters via olfactory/trigeminal nerve; Causes hemorrhagic necrosis of temporal lobes; Asymmetric involvement typical; CSF: Lymphocytic pleocytosis (may be absent early); Elevated protein, normal glucose; RBCs or xanthochromia (hemorrhagic); HSV PCR: 96% sensitivity, 99% specificity; MRI: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in medial temporal lobes; Hemorrhage common; Often asymmetric
HSV encephalitis primarily affects the occipital lobes
Mortality is low even without treatment
CSF is always abnormal from onset

questions.explanation: HSV Encephalitis - The Critical Diagnosis: WHY IT MATTERS: 70% mortality if untreated. Even with treatment, 20-30% mortality. Survivors often have significant neurologic sequelae. CLINICAL FEATURES: Acute onset (days). Fever. Altered mental status - confusion, disorientation. Personality and behavioral changes. Aphasia/dysphasia (temporal lobe). Olfactory or gustatory hallucinations. Seizures - focal or generalized (60-70%). Memory impairment. TEMPORAL LOBE PREDILECTION: HSV enters CNS via olfactory nerve or trigeminal ganglion. Causes hemorrhagic necrosis primarily in medial temporal lobes and orbitofrontal cortex. Typically ASYMMETRIC involvement. CSF FINDINGS: Lymphocytic pleocytosis (10-200 cells/µL typical). ELEVATED protein. NORMAL glucose. RBCs or xanthochromia - reflecting hemorrhagic nature. IMPORTANT: CSF may be normal early (first 24-48 hours) in up to 5-10%. HSV PCR: 96% sensitivity, 99% specificity. May be negative in first 72 hours - REPEAT if high suspicion. IMAGING: MRI is gold standard. T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in medial temporal lobes. Hemorrhage common. Asymmetric typical. CT often normal early.

questions.question: When should acyclovir be started?

options.text: ACYCLOVIR TIMING - CRITICAL: START IMMEDIATELY when encephalitis suspected - DO NOT WAIT for LP or imaging results!; Acyclovir is TIME-CRITICAL life-saving treatment; Every hour of delay worsens outcomes; If LP will be delayed >30 minutes for any reason → Start acyclovir FIRST; Empiric acyclovir should cover ALL suspected encephalitis cases until HSV ruled out; DOSE: Adults: 10 mg/kg IV q8h × 14-21 days; Neonates: 20 mg/kg IV q8h × 21 days (higher dose!); Infuse over 1 hour; Monitor renal function (nephrotoxic); DURATION: Confirmed HSV: 14-21 days; Consider repeat LP with PCR at end of therapy; If PCR still positive → continue treatment
Wait for LP results before starting acyclovir
Wait for MRI confirmation of temporal lobe involvement
Start acyclovir only if HSV PCR is positive

questions.explanation: Acyclovir Timing - The Most Critical Decision: WHEN TO START: IMMEDIATELY when encephalitis is suspected. Do NOT wait for: Lumbar puncture results. Imaging. PCR results. If LP will be delayed >30 minutes → START ACYCLOVIR FIRST. HSV encephalitis is a NEUROLOGIC EMERGENCY. TIME-CRITICAL: Studies show outcomes directly correlate with time to treatment. Every hour of delay increases morbidity and mortality. EMPIRIC TREATMENT: ALL patients with suspected encephalitis should receive empiric acyclovir. Continue until HSV is definitively ruled out. DOSING: ADULTS: 10 mg/kg IV every 8 hours. Infuse over 1 hour (reduces nephrotoxicity). NEONATES: 20 mg/kg IV every 8 hours (higher dose due to higher viral load). MONITORING: Renal function (acyclovir is nephrotoxic). Adequate hydration essential. DURATION: Confirmed HSV encephalitis: 14-21 days. IDSA recommends considering repeat CSF PCR at end of therapy. If PCR still positive → continue treatment until negative. Relapse can occur after completing therapy.

questions.stem: You need to establish a diagnosis.
questions.question: What is the diagnostic approach to encephalitis?

options.text: DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH: 1) LUMBAR PUNCTURE (essential unless contraindicated): Opening pressure (often elevated); Cell count with differential; Protein and glucose; HSV-1 and HSV-2 PCR (ALWAYS); VZV PCR; Consider: Enterovirus, West Nile, other arboviruses based on season/geography; Save extra CSF for future testing; 2) IMAGING: MRI preferred (CT often normal early); T2/FLAIR for temporal lobe changes; DWI for early ischemic changes; 3) EEG: Helpful for seizure detection; PLEDs (periodic lateralized epileptiform discharges) suggest HSV; 4) BLOOD TESTS: CBC, CMP, LFTs; Blood cultures; HIV; Arbovirus serology
CT is the preferred imaging for encephalitis
Lumbar puncture should be delayed until MRI is done
Only HSV-1 PCR is needed

questions.explanation: Diagnostic Approach to Encephalitis: LUMBAR PUNCTURE (Essential): Indications for pre-LP imaging: Immunocompromised. New focal neurologic deficit. New seizure. Papilledema. Altered consciousness. CSF STUDIES: Opening pressure (often elevated). Cell count with differential. Protein and glucose. HSV-1 AND HSV-2 PCR (ALWAYS - 96% sens, 99% spec). VZV PCR. Consider based on epidemiology: Enterovirus PCR. West Nile/arbovirus IgM. Bacterial culture (rule out meningitis). SAVE extra CSF (2-5 mL) for future testing. TYPICAL CSF FINDINGS: Lymphocytic pleocytosis (10-200 cells). Elevated protein. Normal glucose. RBCs may be present (HSV). IMAGING: MRI is PREFERRED - much more sensitive than CT. CT often normal early in HSV encephalitis. MRI findings: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in medial temporal lobes. Hemorrhage (HSV). Asymmetric involvement typical. EEG: Abnormal in >80% of viral encephalitis. PLEDs (periodic lateralized epileptiform discharges) - highly suggestive of HSV. Detects subclinical seizures/non-convulsive status. ADDITIONAL TESTS: Blood cultures. HIV testing. Arbovirus serology (paired acute/convalescent).

questions.stem: The initial HSV PCR comes back negative.
questions.question: When should HSV PCR be repeated?

options.text: NEGATIVE HSV PCR MANAGEMENT (IDSA 2008): If HIGH clinical suspicion + negative initial PCR: CONTINUE acyclovir; REPEAT CSF PCR in 3-7 days; FALSE NEGATIVES occur: Within first 72 hours of symptom onset (viral load too low); Blood in CSF (hemoglobin inhibits PCR); Early in infection before viral replication peaks; WHEN TO STOP ACYCLOVIR if PCR negative: CSF HSV PCR negative AFTER 72 hours of symptoms AND Normal MRI (no temporal lobe changes) AND Normal CSF cell count AND Alternative diagnosis identified or clinical improvement; CAUTION: A single negative PCR does NOT rule out HSV if clinical suspicion remains high
Negative PCR rules out HSV - stop acyclovir immediately
Repeat PCR is never needed
Wait 2 weeks before repeating PCR

questions.explanation: Managing Negative HSV PCR: FALSE NEGATIVE HSV PCR CAUSES: Sample obtained within first 72 hours of symptom onset. Blood in CSF (hemoglobin inhibits PCR). Low viral load early in infection. Technical issues. IDSA 2008 RECOMMENDATIONS: If high clinical suspicion + negative initial PCR: CONTINUE acyclovir empirically. REPEAT LP with CSF PCR in 3-7 days. PCR typically becomes positive during first week. PCR usually becomes negative after 10-14 days of treatment. WHEN IT'S SAFE TO STOP ACYCLOVIR: HSV PCR negative AND obtained >72 hours after symptom onset AND MRI normal (no temporal lobe abnormalities) AND CSF cell count normal AND Alternative diagnosis identified OR clear clinical improvement without HSV features. CRITICAL POINTS: A single negative PCR does NOT definitively rule out HSV encephalitis if clinical suspicion remains high. When in doubt, continue acyclovir and repeat testing. The consequences of missing HSV encephalitis (death or severe disability) far outweigh risks of empiric acyclovir.

questions.question: When should additional agents beyond acyclovir be considered?

options.text: ADDITIONAL COVERAGE CONSIDERATIONS: DOXYCYCLINE 100mg BID: Rocky Mountain spotted fever (tick exposure, rash); Ehrlichiosis/Anaplasmosis (tick exposure); Geographic risk (Southeast, South-central US); Summer/fall season; AMPICILLIN (Listeria coverage): Age >50 years; Immunocompromised; Pregnancy; CSF with normal lactate makes Listeria less likely; BACTERIAL MENINGITIS COVERAGE: If cannot distinguish from bacterial meningitis; Vancomycin + Ceftriaxone + Dexamethasone; Pending CSF results; VZV: Acyclovir covers VZV at same dose; No additional agent needed; CMV (immunocompromised): Ganciclovir + Foscarnet
Acyclovir alone is sufficient for all encephalitis
Antibacterial coverage is never needed
Doxycycline should be given to all patients

questions.explanation: Additional Antimicrobial Coverage: ACYCLOVIR - Covers: HSV-1 and HSV-2. VZV (at same dose - 10 mg/kg IV q8h). DOXYCYCLINE 100mg PO/IV BID - Add when: Tick exposure history. Geographic risk (Southeast, South-central US). Summer/fall season. Skin rash. Covers: Rocky Mountain spotted fever. Ehrlichiosis. Anaplasmosis. Tick-borne encephalitis. AMPICILLIN - Listeria Coverage: Indications: Age >50 years, Immunocompromised (steroids, chemotherapy, transplant), Pregnancy, Alcoholism. Dose: 2g IV q4h. Note: If CSF lactate is normal, Listeria is much less likely. BACTERIAL MENINGITIS COVERAGE: When encephalitis vs meningitis unclear. Vancomycin + Ceftriaxone. Consider dexamethasone (before/with first antibiotic dose). CMV ENCEPHALITIS (Immunocompromised): Ganciclovir 5 mg/kg IV q12h PLUS Foscarnet 60 mg/kg IV q8h. Combination superior to monotherapy. EMPIRIC REGIMEN FOR UNDIFFERENTIATED ENCEPHALITIS: Acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h (HSV/VZV) ± Doxycycline (if tick exposure/endemic area) ± Ampicillin (if risk factors for Listeria) ± Vancomycin/Ceftriaxone (if meningitis not excluded).

questions.question: When should autoimmune encephalitis be considered?

options.text: AUTOIMMUNE ENCEPHALITIS: Consider when: Infectious workup negative; Psychiatric symptoms prominent (psychosis, catatonia); Movement disorders (dyskinesias, dystonia); Seizures refractory to standard therapy; Autonomic instability; Young women (NMDA-R encephalitis); NMDA RECEPTOR ENCEPHALITIS: Most common autoimmune cause; Young women predominantly; Associated with ovarian teratoma (check imaging!); Prodrome: Viral-like illness; Then: Psychiatric symptoms → seizures → movement disorder → autonomic instability → decreased consciousness; WORKUP: CSF NMDA-R antibodies; Serum autoantibody panel; Pelvic imaging (ovarian teratoma); Consider paraneoplastic panel; TREATMENT: Immunotherapy (steroids, IVIG, plasmapheresis); Tumor removal if present; Second-line: Rituximab, cyclophosphamide
Autoimmune encephalitis only occurs in elderly
MRI is always abnormal in autoimmune encephalitis
Psychiatric symptoms exclude autoimmune cause

questions.explanation: Autoimmune Encephalitis: WHEN TO CONSIDER: Infectious workup negative. Prominent psychiatric symptoms. Movement disorders. Refractory seizures. Autonomic instability. Subacute onset (weeks). Young patient, especially female. NMDA RECEPTOR ENCEPHALITIS (Most Common): Demographics: Young women predominantly (but can occur in men/children). Associated with ovarian teratoma in 50% of women. Clinical Progression: Prodrome: Viral-like illness (fever, headache). Psychiatric phase: Anxiety, psychosis, catatonia, behavioral changes. Seizure phase: Often refractory. Movement disorder: Orofacial dyskinesias, choreoathetosis. Autonomic instability: Tachycardia, hyperthermia, blood pressure lability. Decreased consciousness. DIAGNOSTIC WORKUP: CSF: NMDA-R antibodies (more sensitive than serum). Serum: Autoantibody panel. Imaging: MRI often normal (50%), Pelvic ultrasound/CT/MRI (ovarian teratoma). EEG: Extreme delta brush pattern (pathognomonic but rare). OTHER AUTOIMMUNE CAUSES: LGI1 antibody (faciobrachial dystonic seizures). CASPR2, GABA-B, AMPA-R antibodies. Paraneoplastic antibodies (anti-Hu, anti-Ma2). TREATMENT: First-line: High-dose corticosteroids, IVIG, or plasmapheresis. Tumor removal if identified. Second-line: Rituximab, cyclophosphamide.

questions.stem: The patient's family asks about prognosis.
questions.question: What are outcomes in HSV encephalitis?

options.text: HSV ENCEPHALITIS OUTCOMES: WITHOUT treatment: 70% mortality; Most survivors have severe disability; WITH acyclovir treatment: Mortality reduced to 20-30%; Still significant morbidity; SURVIVORS: ~40% develop normally (neonates with high-dose regimen); Memory impairment common (temporal lobe damage); Behavioral changes; Seizure disorders; Cognitive deficits; PROGNOSTIC FACTORS (worse outcomes): Delay in acyclovir initiation; Older age; Lower GCS at presentation; Extensive MRI abnormalities; Immunocompromised status; Positive CSF PCR at end of treatment (associated with relapse); RELAPSE: Can occur after completing therapy; ~8% in neonates treated with standard dose; Less common with high-dose, prolonged therapy; Consider repeat PCR at end of treatment
Most patients fully recover without sequelae
Acyclovir doesn't significantly affect mortality
Age doesn't affect prognosis

questions.explanation: HSV Encephalitis Prognosis: UNTREATED HSV ENCEPHALITIS: 70% mortality. Survivors almost universally have severe neurologic disability. TREATED WITH ACYCLOVIR: Mortality reduced to 20-30%. Significant improvement but still serious. LONG-TERM SEQUELAE IN SURVIVORS: Memory impairment (temporal lobe damage). Behavioral and personality changes. Epilepsy/seizure disorders. Cognitive deficits. Aphasia. Psychiatric symptoms. Only ~40% of neonates develop normally (with optimal treatment). POOR PROGNOSTIC FACTORS: Delay in initiating acyclovir (most important modifiable factor). Older age. Lower GCS at presentation. Extensive MRI abnormalities. Immunocompromised state. Positive CSF PCR at completion of therapy. RELAPSE: Can occur after completing standard therapy. ~8% relapse rate in neonates with 10-day treatment. Reduced with high-dose (20 mg/kg), prolonged (21 days) therapy. IDSA recommends checking CSF PCR at end of therapy. If still positive, continue treatment. FOLLOW-UP: Long-term neurology follow-up essential. Rehabilitation services often needed. Seizure management. Neuropsychological assessment.

questions.question: What is the complete approach to this patient?

options.text: Complete approach: (1) RECOGNIZE ENCEPHALITIS: Fever + altered mental status + seizure + NO meningismus = Encephalitis; (2) START ACYCLOVIR IMMEDIATELY: 10 mg/kg IV q8h - DO NOT WAIT for LP/imaging; This is a NEUROLOGIC EMERGENCY; (3) WORKUP: CT head (before LP if indicated); LP: Cell count, protein, glucose, HSV-1/2 PCR, VZV PCR, cultures; MRI brain (temporal lobes); EEG; (4) ADDITIONAL COVERAGE: Consider doxycycline if tick exposure; Ampicillin if Listeria risk factors; (5) IF PCR NEGATIVE: Continue acyclovir if high suspicion; Repeat LP with PCR in 3-7 days; (6) DURATION: Confirmed HSV: 14-21 days; Consider end-of-treatment PCR; (7) CONSIDER AUTOIMMUNE if infectious workup negative
Wait for MRI before starting treatment
Stop acyclovir immediately if first PCR is negative
7 days of treatment is sufficient

questions.explanation: Complete Management Approach: STEP 1 - RECOGNITION: Encephalitis pattern: Fever, Altered mental status (confusion, personality change), Seizures, NO or minimal meningismus, Focal neurologic signs (aphasia in this case). STEP 2 - IMMEDIATE TREATMENT: START ACYCLOVIR 10 mg/kg IV q8h IMMEDIATELY. Do NOT wait for LP, imaging, or PCR results. HSV encephalitis is a NEUROLOGIC EMERGENCY. Time to treatment directly impacts survival and outcomes. STEP 3 - DIAGNOSTIC WORKUP: CT head (before LP if focal signs, altered consciousness, immunocompromised). Lumbar puncture: Opening pressure, Cell count with differential, Protein and glucose, HSV-1 AND HSV-2 PCR (CRITICAL), VZV PCR, Bacterial culture, Save extra CSF. MRI brain (superior to CT for temporal lobe changes). EEG (seizure detection, PLEDs). STEP 4 - ADDITIONAL COVERAGE: Doxycycline if tick exposure/endemic area. Ampicillin if Listeria risk (age >50, immunocompromised). STEP 5 - MANAGING NEGATIVE PCR: If high clinical suspicion: Continue acyclovir, Repeat LP with PCR in 3-7 days. Safe to stop if: PCR negative after 72h of symptoms + normal MRI + normal CSF + alternative diagnosis. STEP 6 - DURATION: Confirmed HSV: 14-21 days. Consider repeat CSF PCR at end of treatment. If still positive, continue until negative. STEP 7 - BROADEN DIFFERENTIAL: If infectious workup negative, consider autoimmune encephalitis. Check NMDA-R antibodies and pelvic imaging in young women.
